title: Introduction to jobtrackr
text: spring semester senior year. applying jobs weeks, current job market, LinkedIn, referrals, Indeed. spreadsheet mess. remember companies responded, ones ghosted , whether offer NYC actually better one Wisconsin factor cost living. jobtrackr built solve problem. small R package helps track, organize, analyze job applications. find job, help clearer vision ones already applied .

title: Introduction to jobtrackr
text: First, load package built-example dataset. jobs dataset contains 30 simulated job applications across different companies, locations, statuses. think sort realistic semester job hunting looks like. Let us take quick look data contains: row one application. columns tell us company, position, location, cost living index city, current status, date applied, estimated salary, listing found.
code: library(jobtrackr) data(jobs) head(jobs) ##     company         position         location cost_of_living      status ## 1    Google     Data Analyst      New York NY            100 Interviewed ## 2    Amazon   HR Coordinator        Austin TX             82    Rejected ## 3  Deloitte Business Analyst       Chicago IL             88     Applied ## 4 Microsoft   Data Scientist       Seattle WA             91     Offered ## 5     Apple Jr. Statistician San Francisco CA            110    Rejected ## 6       IBM    Data Engineer        Austin TX             82 Interviewed ##   date_applied salary      source ## 1   2024-01-10  95000    LinkedIn ## 2   2024-01-15  85000    Referral ## 3   2024-01-20  78000      Indeed ## 4   2024-02-01 110000    LinkedIn ## 5   2024-02-05  88000 Career Fair ## 6   2024-02-10  82000      Indeed

title: Introduction to jobtrackr
text: first thing job seeker wants know simple: everything applied , actually happened? application_summary() answers question one line. 30 applications, exactly one third still sitting “Applied” response, another third ended rejection. 3 applications (10%) resulted offer. overall response rate 66.7%, meaning roughly 2 every 3 applications received kind reply. kind summary helps quickly understand job search stands without scrolling spreadsheet.
code: application_summary(jobs) ## Response rate (non-Applied): 66.7% ##        status count percent ## 1     Applied    10    33.3 ## 2    Rejected    10    33.3 ## 3 Interviewed     7    23.3 ## 4     Offered     3    10.0

what: Step 2: Who Has Not Responded Yet?
title: Introduction to jobtrackr
text: Waiting hardest part job hunting. difference patiently waiting ghosted. days_since_applied() calculates many days passed since application submitted, flags gone 21 days response. Ten applications gone 21 days response flagged possibly ghosted. Companies like Deloitte, Meta, Salesforce waiting longest, 800 days simulated dataset. real job search, think helpful tells exactly applications either follow move . Knowing ghosted far better keep wonderig statu.s
code: result <- days_since_applied(jobs, flag_after = 21) result[result$possibly_ghosted == TRUE,        c("company", "position", "date_applied", "days_waiting")] ##       company             position date_applied days_waiting ## 3    Deloitte     Business Analyst   2024-01-20          860 ## 7        Meta      Product Analyst   2024-02-15          834 ## 10 Salesforce           BI Analyst   2024-03-05          815 ## 13     Target Supply Chain Analyst   2024-03-12          808 ## 17        PWC      Audit Associate   2024-03-22          798 ## 19     Boeing         Data Analyst   2024-03-28          792 ## 22       KPMG     Advisory Analyst   2024-04-05          784 ## 25    Spotify         Data Analyst   2024-04-12          777 ## 27   LinkedIn            Recruiter   2024-04-17          772 ## 30       Zoom     Customer Success   2024-04-25          764

title: Introduction to jobtrackr
text: received offers! Congrats! higher salary always mean better deal. $95,000 salary NYC buys far less $85,000 WI compare_offers() adjusts offer cost living index city, can compare apples apples. first glance, Microsoft’s offer $110,000 looks like clear winner. adjusted Seattle’s cost living index 91, real purchasing power comes $120,879, still best. Accenture’s $95,000 Chicago adjusts $107,955, Nike’s $75,000 Portland adjusts $88,235. Without adjustment, might overvalue high nominal salary expensive city undervalue modest offer somewhere affordable.
code: compare_offers(jobs) ##     company              position    location cost_of_living  status ## 1 Microsoft        Data Scientist  Seattle WA             91 Offered ## 2 Accenture Management Consultant  Chicago IL             88 Offered ## 3      Nike            HR Analyst Portland OR             85 Offered ##   date_applied salary      source adjusted_salary ## 1   2024-02-01 110000    LinkedIn          120879 ## 2   2024-03-20  95000    Referral          107955 ## 3   2024-03-01  75000 Career Fair           88235

title: Introduction to jobtrackr
text: Finally, pipeline_stage() gives visual summary entire job search funnel — many applications made stage.  chart shows biggest drop-happens Applied Interviewed, 7 30 applications (23%) led interview. , 3 7 interviews (43%) resulted offer. tells hardest part job search getting door first place. want offers, ’s convincing apply strategically, just broadly.

title: Introduction to jobtrackr
text: jobtrackr help ypu prep interviews. give yu broader view job search show clearly working .

title: Authors and Citation
text: Tiana Ho. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Ho T (2026). jobtrackr: Job Applications Tracking Analyzing Tool. R package version 0.1.0, https://adc-405-s26.github.io/jobtrackeR/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {jobtrackr: Job Applications Tracking and Analyzing Tool},   author = {Tiana Ho},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://adc-405-s26.github.io/jobtrackeR/}, }

what: Job Applications Tracking and Analyzing Tool
title: Job Applications Tracking and Analyzing Tool
text: Job hunting daunting, especially market nowadays. dozens applications, different statuses, offers different cities, easy lose track everything . jobtrackr provides four simple tools help organize analyze job search.

title: Job Applications Tracking and Analyzing Tool
Job Applications Tracking and Analyzing Tool
text: application_summary() - counts applications status returns overall response rate days_since_applied() - calculates long application waiting flags ones response set number days compare_offers() - adjusts offer salaries cost living can compare fairly across cities pipeline_stage() - visualizes full hiring funnel Applied Offered

title: Job Applications Tracking and Analyzing Tool
text: Note: GitHub repo named jobtrackeR package loads jobtrackr
code: library(jobtrackr) data(jobs) application_summary(jobs) #>        status count percent #> 1     Applied    10    33.3 #> 2    Rejected    10    33.3 #> 3 Interviewed     7    23.3 #> 4     Offered     3    10.0

title: Job Applications Tracking and Analyzing Tool
text: package includes jobs, simulated dataset 30 job applications columns company, position, location, cost living, status, date applied, salary, source.
code: head(jobs[, c("company", "position", "status", "salary")]) #>     company         position      status salary #> 1    Google     Data Analyst Interviewed  95000 #> 2    Amazon   HR Coordinator    Rejected  85000 #> 3  Deloitte Business Analyst     Applied  78000 #> 4 Microsoft   Data Scientist     Offered 110000 #> 5     Apple Jr. Statistician    Rejected  88000 #> 6       IBM    Data Engineer Interviewed  82000

title: Job Applications Tracking and Analyzing Tool
text: full walkthrough real examples commentary, see Introduction jobtrackr vignette.

title: Summarize job application statuses — application_summary
text: Returns summary table showing count percentage applications status category, along overall response rate.

title: Summarize job application statuses — application_summary
Summarize job application statuses — application_summary
text: df data frame containing job application data. status_col Character. Name column containing application status values. Default "status".

title: Summarize job application statuses — application_summary
text: data frame columns status, count, percent.

title: Summarize job application statuses — application_summary
code: application_summary(jobs) #> Response rate (non-Applied): 66.7% #>        status count percent #> 1     Applied    10    33.3 #> 2    Rejected    10    33.3 #> 3 Interviewed     7    23.3 #> 4     Offered     3    10.0 application_summary(jobs, status_col = "status") #> Response rate (non-Applied): 66.7% #>        status count percent #> 1     Applied    10    33.3 #> 2    Rejected    10    33.3 #> 3 Interviewed     7    23.3 #> 4     Offered     3    10.0

what: Compare job offers adjusted for cost of living — compare_offers
title: Compare job offers adjusted for cost of living — compare_offers
text: Filters applications "Offered" status computes cost--living adjusted salary , allowing fair comparison across different locations.

title: Compare job offers adjusted for cost of living — compare_offers
Compare job offers adjusted for cost of living — compare_offers
text: df data frame containing job application data. status_col Character. Name status column. Default "status". salary_col Character. Name salary column. Default "salary". col_col Character. Name cost living index column. Default "cost_of_living". value 100 represents US average.

title: Compare job offers adjusted for cost of living — compare_offers
text: data frame offered positions added adjusted_salary column, sorted highest lowest adjusted salary.

title: Compare job offers adjusted for cost of living — compare_offers
code: compare_offers(jobs) #>     company              position    location cost_of_living  status #> 1 Microsoft        Data Scientist  Seattle WA             91 Offered #> 2 Accenture Management Consultant  Chicago IL             88 Offered #> 3      Nike            HR Analyst Portland OR             85 Offered #>   date_applied salary      source adjusted_salary #> 1   2024-02-01 110000    LinkedIn          120879 #> 2   2024-03-20  95000    Referral          107955 #> 3   2024-03-01  75000 Career Fair           88235

what: Calculate days since each application was submitted — days_since_applied
title: Calculate days since each application was submitted — days_since_applied
text: Computes number days application date today (reference date), flags applications received response specified number days.

title: Calculate days since each application was submitted — days_since_applied
code: days_since_applied(   df,   date_col = "date_applied",   status_col = "status",   flag_after = 21,   reference_date = Sys.Date() )

title: Calculate days since each application was submitted — days_since_applied
text: df data frame containing job application data. date_col Character. Name column containing application dates. Must class Date. Default "date_applied". status_col Character. Name status column. Default "status". flag_after Numeric. Number days "Applied" application considered potentially ghosted. Default 21. reference_date Date object calculate days . Defaults Sys.Date().

title: Calculate days since each application was submitted — days_since_applied
text: original data frame two added columns: days_waiting possibly_ghosted (logical).

title: Calculate days since each application was submitted — days_since_applied

title: Job Application Tracking Dataset — jobs
text: simulated dataset 30 job applications submitted semester-long job search, containing application status, salary, location, cost living, source information.

title: Job Application Tracking Dataset — jobs
Job Application Tracking Dataset — jobs
text: data frame 30 rows 8 variables: company Character. Name company applied . position Character. Job title applied position. location Character. City state job location. cost_of_living Numeric. Cost living index 100 equals US average. status Character. Current application status: Applied, Interviewed, Offered, Rejected. date_applied Date. date application submitted. salary Numeric. Advertised estimated annual salary USD. source Character. Platform used find job: LinkedIn, Indeed, Referral, Career Fair.

title: Job Application Tracking Dataset — jobs
text: Simulated data created demonstration purposes.

what: Visualize the job application pipeline — pipeline_stage
title: Visualize the job application pipeline — pipeline_stage
text: Creates bar chart showing many applications reached stage hiring process, Applied Offered. Helps identify drop-occurs job search.

title: Visualize the job application pipeline — pipeline_stage
Visualize the job application pipeline — pipeline_stage
text: df data frame containing job application data. status_col Character. Name status column. Default "status".

title: Visualize the job application pipeline — pipeline_stage
text: ggplot2 bar chart object showing application counts stage.

title: Visualize the job application pipeline — pipeline_stage
